title: How Did Everything Begin?: Animations on the Origins of the Universe Narrated by X‑Files Star Gillian Anderson
description:
in Philosophy, Religion, Science |   	July 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We pre­vi­ous­ly brought you the BBC series of short ani­mat­ed vid
body:
in Philosophy, Religion, Science |   	July 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

We pre­vi­ous­ly brought you the BBC series of short ani­mat­ed videos, A His­to­ry of Ideas. Pro­duced in col­lab­o­ra­tion with the UK’s Open Uni­ver­si­ty and nar­rat­ed by Har­ry Shear­er, these fun intro­duc­tions to such philoso­phers as Simone de Beau­voir and Edmund Burke, and such weighty philo­soph­i­cal top­ics as free will and the prob­lem of evil, make chal­leng­ing, abstract con­cepts acces­si­ble to non-philoso­phers. Now the series is back with a new chap­ter, How Did Every­thing Begin?, a sur­vey of sev­er­al the­o­ries of the ori­gins of the uni­verse, from Thomas Aquinas’ philo­soph­i­cal spec­u­la­tions, to Hin­du cos­mol­o­gy; and from the­olo­gian William Paley’s design argu­ment (below), to the the­o­ry of the Big Bang (above).


	

The two videos here present an inter­est­ing coun­ter­point between the ori­gin the­o­ries of astro­physics and the­ol­o­gy. Though cur­rent-day intel­li­gent design pro­po­nents deny it, there is still much of William Paley’s argu­ment, at least in style, in their expla­na­tions of cre­ation. First pro­pound­ed in his 1802 work Nat­ur­al The­ol­o­gy, the theologian’s famous watch­mak­er analogy—which he extend­ed to the design of the eye, and every­thing else—gave Charles Dar­win much to puz­zle over, though David Hume had sup­pos­ed­ly refut­ed Paley’s argu­ments 50 years ear­li­er. The Big Bang theory—a term cre­at­ed by its fore­most crit­ic Fred Hoyle—offers an entire­ly nat­u­ral­is­tic account of the universe’s ori­gins, one that pre­sup­pos­es no inher­ent pur­pose or design.
As with the pre­vi­ous videos, these are script­ed by the host of the Phi­los­o­phy Bites pod­cast, Nigel War­bur­ton. This time around the videos are nar­rat­ed by Gillian Ander­son, whose voice you may not imme­di­ate­ly rec­og­nize. Rather than sound­ing like Dana Scul­ly, her famous X‑Files char­ac­ter, Ander­son speaks in a British accent, which she slips into eas­i­ly, hav­ing lived in the UK for much of her child­hood and now again as an adult. (You may have seen Ander­son in many of the Eng­lish peri­od dra­mas she has appeared in, or in British crime dra­ma The Fall or Michael Winterbottom’s uproar­i­ous adap­ta­tion of Tris­tram Shandy.)
These fas­ci­nat­ing spec­u­la­tive theories—whether sci­en­tif­ic or mythological—are sure to appeal to fans of the X‑Files, who can per­haps begin to believe again, or remain skep­ti­cal, thanks to Chris Carter’s R‑rated direc­tor’s cut of X‑Files: I Want to Believe, com­ing out this fall.
Watch the remain­ing videos in the series below:
Thomas Aquinas and the First Mover Argu­ment


	

Hin­du Cre­ation Sto­ries


	

Note: An ear­li­er ver­sion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
Relat­ed Con­tent:
A His­to­ry of Ideas: Ani­mat­ed Videos Explain The­o­ries of Simone de Beau­voir, Edmund Burke & Oth­er Philoso­phers
The His­to­ry of Phi­los­o­phy With­out Any Gaps – Peter Adamson’s Pod­cast Still Going Strong
Free Online Phi­los­o­phy Cours­es
Josh Jones is a writer and musi­cian based in Durham, NC.

schema.headline: How Did Everything Begin?: Animations on the Origins of the Universe Narrated by X‑Files Star Gillian Anderson

title: Umberto Eco’s Personal Library Opens to the Public: More Than 32,000 Books, Exactly as He Left Them
description: Umber­to Eco’s The Name of the Rose sold so well that its Eng­lish trans­la­tor William Weaver could add an exten­sion onto his Tus­can vil­l
body:
Umber­to Eco’s The Name of the Rose sold so well that its Eng­lish trans­la­tor William Weaver could add an exten­sion onto his Tus­can vil­la, which he called the “Eco Cham­ber.” The Bib­liote­ca Eco, or Eco Library, whose rib­bon-cut­ting ear­li­er this month you can see in the Ital­ian news clip just above, may not be as wit­ti­ly named, but it’s a much more ambi­tious project. It was opened this year, the tenth anniver­sary of the famed philoso­pher, semi­oti­cian, nov­el­ist, and crit­ic’s death, at the Uni­ver­si­ty of Bologna, where he was a pro­fes­sor from 1971 to 2007. Hous­ing more than 32,000 books from his per­son­al library in Milan (pre­vi­ous­ly fea­tured here on Open Cul­ture, and the sub­ject of its own fea­ture-length doc­u­men­tary), it stands as an explorable con­crete rep­re­sen­ta­tion of his capa­cious mind.
So capa­cious was that mind, in fact, that the Eco Library actu­al­ly com­pris­es sev­er­al libraries, chiefly the Rare Book Library and the much-sub­di­vid­ed Work­ing Library. In them, one encoun­ters sec­tions “ded­i­cat­ed to lit­er­ary gen­res, comics and pop­u­lar cul­ture, works by and about Umber­to Eco” — includ­ing more than 2,000 edi­tions and trans­la­tions of his own books — “ancient and medieval phi­los­o­phy, the his­to­ry of ideas, semi­otics, trans­la­tion and the­o­ries of lan­guage,” says the Uni­ver­si­ty of Bolog­na’s mag­a­zine Uni­bo.
There are “Ital­ian, French, Eng­lish-lan­guage, Ger­man, Cen­tral Euro­pean, Slav­ic, Latin Amer­i­can and many oth­er lit­er­a­tures,” includ­ing works of “detec­tive fic­tion and sci­ence fic­tion, pop­u­lar lit­er­a­ture and the feuil­leton.” As any­one who’s read Eco’s essay on Peanuts might imag­ine, “his com­ic book col­lec­tion is also of great impor­tance.”
Indeed, as any fan would expect, Eco’s name­sake library reflects not just the vast sweep of his human­is­tic inter­ests and knowl­edge, but also his play­ful sense of eccen­tric­i­ty, and even grotes­querie. Accord­ing to the Fon­dazione Umber­to Eco, most of the vol­umes it con­tains “are marked by a dis­tinc­tive ex lib­ris,” an adap­ta­tion of a six­teenth-cen­tu­ry wood­cut that depicts a don­key on a cor­nu­copia-topped latrine. “The ani­mal plays a small pipe, set­ting in motion a mer­ry dance of a dozen cavort­ing mon­keys.” The books them­selves have been painstak­ing­ly arranged just as Eco shelved them, in accor­dance with the “good neigh­bor” prin­ci­ple of plac­ing seem­ing­ly unre­lat­ed titles in close prox­im­i­ty in order to reveal unex­pect­ed intel­lec­tu­al con­nec­tions between them. The vol­umes from Eco’s coun­try house in Monte Cerignone will also be incor­po­rat­ed into the col­lec­tion, but as the man him­self knew, the best libraries, like the best minds, are nev­er tru­ly com­plete.
Relat­ed con­tent:
Watch Umber­to Eco Walk Through His Immense Pri­vate Library: It Goes On, and On, and On!
Umber­to Eco Explains Why We Make Lists
The Virtue of Own­ing Books You Haven’t Read: Why Umber­to Eco Kept an “Antili­brary”
Dis­cov­er the 1126 Books in John Cage’s Per­son­al Library: Fou­cault, Joyce, Wittgen­stein, Vir­ginia Woolf, Buck­min­ster Fuller & More
Darwin’s Per­son­al Library Goes Dig­i­tal: 330 Books Online
The Largest Book­shelf Tour Ever Filmed: Inside a Classicist’s 20,000-Volume Library
Based in Seoul, Col­in Marshall writes and broad­casts on cities, lan­guage, and cul­ture. He’s the author of the newslet­ter Books on Cities as well as the books 한국 요약 금지 (No Sum­ma­riz­ing Korea) and Kore­an Newtro. Fol­low him on the social net­work for­mer­ly known as Twit­ter at @colinmarshall.

title: Where Did Europe’s Countries—and the United Kingdom—Get Their Names?
description:
in History, Language |   	July 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The typ­i­cal stu­dent trav­el­ing on a Eurail pass will stop in such lands as
body:
in History, Language |   	July 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The typ­i­cal stu­dent trav­el­ing on a Eurail pass will stop in such lands as Öster­re­ich, Italia, Česko and Schweiz — also known as Suisse, Svizzera, and Svizra. If that stu­dent hap­pens to hail from the Eng­lish-speak­ing world, the names Aus­tria, Italy, the Czech Repub­lic (the recent move­ment for Czechia notwith­stand­ing), and Switzer­land will sound more famil­iar. But the ques­tion inevitably comes to mind while rid­ing between one his­tor­i­cal­ly and cul­tur­al­ly rich cap­i­tal to anoth­er: where did all these coun­tries get their names in Eng­lish? How on Earth, for exam­ple, did Deutsch­land, known in cer­tain oth­er Euro­pean lan­guages as Alle­magne, Ale­ma­nia, or Ale­man­ha, get to be called Ger­many?
Lan­guage YouTu­ber Rob­Words has an expla­na­tion. “To the Romans, it was the land of the Ger­mani, their name for the weird peo­ple speak­ing gib­ber­ish lan­guages to the east of the Riv­er Rhine,” he says in the video above, stand­ing in a for­est con­ve­nient­ly locat­ed in Ger­many itself.
They “also used the word ale­man­ni to describe a con­fed­er­a­tion of Ger­man­ic peo­ple, and that’s where a load of oth­er lan­guages get their word for Ger­many too.” And the mean­ing of Deutsch­land? “Land of the Dutch,” that last actu­al­ly being the descen­dant of an old Ger­man­ic word mean­ing ‘pop­u­lar’ or ‘peo­ple’ ” — which, before that time, referred not sim­ply to human beings, but the pop­u­la­tion who spoke the region’s wide­ly under­stood “pop­u­lar lan­guages.”


	

Behind most every Euro­pean coun­try’s Eng­lish name lies a sto­ry, if some­times an ambigu­ous, con­test­ed, and con­tro­ver­sial one. Most are also his­tor­i­cal­ly illu­mi­nat­ing: take Bel­gium, once con­sid­ered part of the Nether­lands (home of the peo­ple we now call Dutch), whose name derives from a tribe, or con­fed­er­a­tion of tribes, known in Latin as the Bel­gae: a word “adopt­ed by peo­ple liv­ing in the south­ern Low Coun­tries dur­ing the region’s strug­gle for inde­pen­dence from Spain in the six­teenth cen­tu­ry.” Rob­Words has more such sur­pris­ing facts to reveal in the video just above, in which he breaks down the names of all the coun­tries in the Unit­ed King­dom, cov­er­ing Ire­land, Scot­land, Wales, his native Eng­land, and also the word Britain itself, as well as the Great often attached to it. The lat­ter isn’t a boast, he stress­es, but rather a means of dis­tin­guish­ing the island from part of France. As for which part, you’ll have to watch to find out.
Relat­ed Con­tent:
A Map Shows What Every Coun­try in the World Calls Itself in its Own Lan­guage: Explore the “Endonyms of the World” Map
The Sur­pris­ing Pat­tern Behind the Names of Col­ors Around the World
A Map of How the Word “Tea” Spread Across the World
Wel­come to Llan­fair­p­wll­gwyn­gyll­gogerych­wyrn­drob­wl­l­l­lan­tysil­i­o­gogogoc, the Town with the Longest Name in Europe
The His­to­ry of Europe from 400 BC to the Present, Ani­mat­ed in 12 Min­utes
The Entire His­to­ry of Eng­lish in 22 Min­utes
Based in Seoul, Col­in Marshall writes and broad­casts on cities, lan­guage, and cul­ture. He’s the author of the newslet­ter Books on Cities as well as the books 한국 요약 금지 (No Sum­ma­riz­ing Korea) and Kore­an Newtro. Fol­low him on the social net­work for­mer­ly known as Twit­ter at @colinmarshall.

title: A Free Online Archive Lets You Explore 5.8 Million Works of Art Spanning 5,000 Years of History
description:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	July 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Though it’s tra­di­tion­al­ly meant “last in a pro­gres­sion or series,” as Me
body:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	July 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Though it’s tra­di­tion­al­ly meant “last in a pro­gres­sion or series,” as Mer­ri­am-Web­ster puts it, the word ulti­mate has been more often used in recent decades to mean “the best or most extreme of its kind.” The name of a recent­ly launched dig­i­tal archive per­forms a rare inver­sion, label­ing as last a cre­ation marked not by final­i­ty but extrem­i­ty. Billed as “the largest search engine for muse­um art,” The Last Muse­um cur­rent­ly index­es around five mil­lion art­works, both those in the pub­lic domain and those cov­ered by fair use. What­ev­er sort of art you wish to see, just describe it in the search bar, hit enter, and enjoy the numer­ous and diverse results from through­out his­to­ry and all around the world.

“Rather than func­tion­ing as a muse­um itself, the web­site aggre­gates pub­licly avail­able col­lec­tion records and images from thou­sands of insti­tu­tions, allow­ing vis­i­tors to explore every­thing from Old Mas­ter paint­ings and ancient Egypt­ian sculp­ture to Japan­ese wood­block prints and vin­tage comics in one place,” writes Sage Helene at My Mod­ern Met.
“Vis­i­tors can search by themes, moods, col­ors, sub­jects, or descrip­tive phras­es with­out know­ing an artwork’s title or artist. The sys­tem iden­ti­fies relat­ed con­cepts and returns visu­al­ly and the­mat­i­cal­ly con­nect­ed works, mak­ing it easy to dis­cov­er unex­pect­ed pieces across cul­tures, time peri­ods, and col­lec­tions.” Its cre­ators under­stand that the val­ue of search lies in not just effi­cien­cy, but also sur­prise — not that the user expe­ri­ence is entire­ly “vibes-based,” as the kids today might say.

Though the search results can come, by default, from any time between 3000 BC and the cur­rent year, you can nar­row the his­tor­i­cal range by drag­ging the ends of the blue time­line inward. And if you pre­fer more spe­cif­ic brows­ing, you can also nav­i­gate through its lists of cul­tures or muse­ums.  The for­mer includes every­thing from French, Roman, and Japan­ese (with a strong selec­tion of wood­block prints), to Cham­pa, Tajik, and Basque. The lat­ter includes the British Muse­um, the Rijksmu­se­um, and the Met­ro­pol­i­tan Muse­um of Art, as well as the likes of the HNM-PCC Hun­gar­i­an Nation­al Muse­um, the Turin Nation­al Uni­ver­si­ty Library, and the Bath Postal Muse­um: insti­tu­tions you can nev­er be sure when you’ll vis­it next. There will, of course, be more muse­ums found­ed in the future. But if things go accord­ing to plan, none of their hold­ings will escape The Last Muse­um’s ever-widen­ing dig­i­tal net. Enter the dig­i­tal col­lec­tion here.
via MyMod­ern­Met
Relat­ed con­tent:
Explore the New­ly Launched Pub­lic Domain Image Archive with 10,000+ Free His­tor­i­cal Images
Public.Work: A Smooth­ly Search­able Archive of 100,000+ “Copy­right-Free” Images
A Search Engine for Find­ing Free, Pub­lic Domain Images from World-Class Muse­ums
The Get­ty Makes Near­ly 88,000 Art Images Free to Use How­ev­er You Like
Down­load Great Works of Art from 40+ Muse­ums World­wide: Explore Artvee, the New Art Search Engine
Cre­ative Com­mons Offi­cial­ly Launch­es a Search Engine That Index­es 300+ Mil­lion Pub­lic Domain Images
Based in Seoul, Col­in Marshall writes and broad­casts on cities, lan­guage, and cul­ture. He’s the author of the newslet­ter Books on Cities as well as the books 한국 요약 금지 (No Sum­ma­riz­ing Korea) and Kore­an Newtro. Fol­low him on the social net­work for­mer­ly known as Twit­ter at @colinmarshall.

title: 19th Century Japanese Woodblock Prints Creatively Illustrate the Inner Workings of the Human Body
description:
in Art |   	July 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Folks with a pass­ing knowl­edge of ukiyo‑e, the Japan­ese wood­block print art form pop­u­lar in
body:
in Art |   	July 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Folks with a pass­ing knowl­edge of ukiyo‑e, the Japan­ese wood­block print art form pop­u­lar in the 17th through 19th cen­turies, will be famil­iar with its land­scapes, as well as its por­traits of cour­te­sans and kabu­ki actors. But often these prints were edu­ca­tion­al, as demon­strat­ed by these very odd anatom­i­cal prints that pro­mote good health as it relates to our inter­nal work­ings.
Long before ani­mat­ed mon­sters warned us about our mucus-filled chests, Japan­ese artists like Uta­gawa Kunisa­da (1786–1865) filled the guts of these men and women with lit­tle work­ers, mak­ing sure the human body worked like a func­tion­ing vil­lage or town.
In the first print, Inshoku Yojo Kaga­mi (“Mir­ror of the Phys­i­ol­o­gy of Drink­ing and Eat­ing”), a man dines on fish and drinks sake. Inside, lit­tle men scur­ry about a pool wrapped in intestines, stoke a fire under the heart, all the while a schol­ar keeps ref­er­ence mate­ri­als near­by. Down below lone­ly fig­ures guard the “urine gate” and the “feces gate,” sure­ly one of the worst jobs in all the body econ­o­my.

One of Kunisada’s stu­dents cre­at­ed a print for the women, focus­ing on the repro­duc­tive organs, called Boji Yojo Kaga­mi (“Rules of Sex­u­al Life”). Keen-eyed view­ers will note that the minia­ture work­ers here are all women, so at least there’s some equal­i­ty at play.

The two prints were meant as instruc­tion­al, point­ing out best health prac­tices, and warn­ing against overindul­gence and excess.

Oth­er prints are just as inven­tive: a back and abdomen cov­ered in chil­dren play­ing famil­iar games; anoth­er fea­tur­ing pop­u­lar kabu­ki actors stand­ing in for var­i­ous organs. (Now, that is just cry­ing out for a mod­ern remake). The last print shows a preg­nant woman whose bel­ly con­tains Tainai jukkai no zu (Ten Realms with­in the body), a Bud­dhist idea that you can read more about here. As for their func­tion inside the womb, that is for oth­ers of a high­er con­scious­ness to dis­cern.

Note: An ear­li­er ver­sion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
Relat­ed Con­tent:
Map­ping Emo­tions in the Body: A Finnish Neu­ro­science Study Reveals Where We Feel Emo­tions in Our Bod­ies
A Sub­way Map of Human Anato­my: All the Sys­tems of Our Body Visu­al­ized in the Style of the Lon­don Under­ground
“Man as Indus­tri­al Palace,” the 1926 Lith­o­graph Depict­ing the Human Body as a Mod­ern Fac­to­ry, Comes to Life in a New Ani­ma­tion
 Ted Mills is a free­lance writer on the arts.

schema.headline: 19th Century Japanese Woodblock Prints Creatively Illustrate the Inner Workings of the Human Body

title: Playful Knitted Pillows by Donna Wilson Infuse Interiors with Whimsy and Joy
description:
All images courtesy of Donna Wilson, shared with permission
July 23, 2026


For Donna Wilson, there’s no reason that knitwear and home decor shouldn’
body:
All images courtesy of Donna Wilson, shared with permission
July 23, 2026


For Donna Wilson, there’s no reason that knitwear and home decor shouldn’t incorporate a lot of color and elicit joy. From patterned towels and socks to blankets and hot water bottle sleeves, the London-based designer celebrates vibrant patterns and playful creatures, especially in her series of cushions. What’s not to love about a pillow with personality?
The soft objects ranging from bolsters to floor cushions to throw pillows are covered in knitted lambswool. Current designs include a larger-than-life banana, a whale, a toadstool, and more. The objects are produced in small batches in what Wilson describes as a “micro-factory” called the Knit Shop in Scotland, where vibrant flora and fauna, not to mention foods and playful takes on human body parts, are conceived as squishy instances of delight. See more in her shop.

schema.headline: Playful Knitted Pillows by Donna Wilson Infuse Interiors with Whimsy and Joy

title: Patrick Dougherty's 'Stickwork' Installations Conjure Connections with Nature
description: For more than four decades, environmental artist Patrick Dougherty has reimagined the technical and sculptural capabilities of one simple material
body:
For more than four decades, environmental artist Patrick Dougherty has reimagined the technical and sculptural capabilities of one simple material: twigs. Over the years, the North Carolina-based artist completed well over 300 large-scale, site-specific installations in a series he calls Stickworks, using literal truckloads of willow saplings from specialized farms in Canada that grow them for applications like fencing, basketmaking, and more. Where possible, Dougherty has also incorporated materials the local area had to offer.
I first stumbled upon the artist’s whimsical constructions in Door County, Wisconsin, where “Happy Go Lucky” sits at Horseshoe Bay Farms near Egg Harbor. By the time I experienced the work, it had already been colonized by Virginia creeper vines, which turn bright red in the autumn. The piece is simultaneously structural and fantastical, providing an inviting armature for nature, including insects.
“The Jealous Wall.” Image courtesy of Docent
Some of Dougherty’s public installations take architectural forms consisting of arches and towers, such as “Sea Change” and “Rough ‘n Tumble.” “All in the Family” resembles a group of larger-than-life urns. And others lean into a sense that the forest is always in a state of reclaiming. “The Jealous Wall” is constructed around existing buildings, as if the saplings are growing in slow motion and and engulfing everything in its path, not unlike the way nature, left to its own devices, will always redeem its space. All of his works emphasize an invitation, as visitors are encouraged to walk around and through the sculptures.
Pieces created over the past decade or so also bear the signature of artist Sam Dougherty, who provided full-time assistance in addition to his own pottery practice. Semi-retired now, Patrick Dougherty is still making works in his studio, and a recent exhibition at the Brahm Blowing Rock Art & History Museum included a wall-to-wall display of gloves collected over the years and signed by the volunteers who helped to install the works. Explore more from the archive on the artist’s website.
“Happy Go Lucky.” Image courtesy of Horseshoe Bay Farms
“Hopscotch.” Image courtesy of The Wild Center
“Grand Center.” Photo by Bob Joy
“Fly Away Home.” Photo by Kurt Hilton Photography
“Finders Keepers.” Photo by Sam Pope
“All in the Family.” Photo by Blake Marvin
Detail of “All in the Family.” Photo by Blake Marvin
“Sea Change.” Photo by Brian Janssen
“Sea Change.” Photo by Brian Janssen
“Hopscotch.” Image courtesy of The Wild Center

schema.headline: Patrick Dougherty's 'Stickwork' Installations Conjure Connections with Nature
